caveats: a: Occupation evidence is for NAICS 311 and mixes very different food processes.
a: Advanced-technology exposure includes robotics, specialized software, and equipment rather than isolating AI.
rho: The BTOS question now covers AI in any business function, broadening measured use.
rho: Survey adoption does not measure the fraction of exposed opportunity actually captured.
e: No source measures eligibility within the frozen 43-firm estimate.
e: The margin bridge used to estimate the band may misclassify branded, retail-heavy, or volatile-margin firms.
s5: The evidence does not isolate this NAICS or EBITDA band.
s5: Stated plans are not completed transactions and include transfer forms outside the qualifying definition.
q: Large-brand economics do not represent smaller converters.
q: Observed price realization responds mainly to cocoa and other cost inflation, not AI productivity.
d5: The category mixes multiple NAICS codes and retail margins.
d5: The constant-price conversion is judgmental because the cited forecast is nominal.
o: The estimate excludes storefront-led manufacturers through the lens narrowing.
o: Consolidation into larger suppliers preserves operator-required demand but may remove it from the LMM population.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat finished-product sales priced per unit, case, shipment, or production run, often through private-label programs, wholesale catalogs, distributor relationships, seasonal orders, and recurring branded accounts.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS definition mixes industrial manufacturers with establishments retailing chocolate confectionery made on premises. The lens retains recurring external-customer manufacturing and excludes storefront-led retail because their labor mix, customer acquisition, pricing, and transferability are materially different.
lens.excluded_activities: Storefront-led on-premises retail confectioners, immediate-consumption foodservice, cacao-bean processors, nonchocolate candy makers, pure retailers, captive plants, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers that convert purchased chocolate into finished confectionery for recurring wholesale, private-label, corporate-gifting, foodservice, distributor, or retailer customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across all food manufacturing rather than purchased-chocolate confectionery, while Census measures upper-bound worker exposure to all advanced automating technologies in manufacturing. I map AI to planning, machine-vision inspection, order entry, formulation documentation, scheduling, customer service, and administrative tasks, then discount physical molding, enrobing, decorating, packaging, sanitation, maintenance, and material handling. The broad automation measure likely overstates AI-only exposure but the broad food mix may understate small-plant office multitasking.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for NAICS 311 and mixes very different food processes.
Advanced-technology exposure includes robotics, specialized software, and equipment rather than isolating AI.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted six-digit occupation matrix plus task diaries from independent private-label and wholesale confectioners, including current use of vision systems and planning software, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Purchased-chocolate manufacturers avoid bean processing but still depend on hands-on forming, filling, decorating, cooling, packaging, sanitation, and quality control. AI can substitute portions of cognitive and inspection workflows, not most physical production tasks.

d5.bridge: NCA measures the entire US confectionery category and forecasts nominal retail sales from $55 billion in 2025 to $62.2 billion in 2030, while reporting 99.8% household penetration and that inflation lifted dollars as unit and volume sales struggled. BLS projects food-processing-equipment-worker employment up 5% over 2024-2034. I remove nominal price growth and use half the broad-food ten-year employment signal as only a loose quantity anchor; both sources are broader than purchased-chocolate manufacturing, and nominal forecasts overstate real demand.
caveats: The category mixes multiple NAICS codes and retail margins.
The constant-price conversion is judgmental because the cited forecast is nominal.
d5.change_evidence: Circana or NIQ five-year unit data and Euromonitor constant-price forecasts specifically for purchased-chocolate confectionery, separated by branded, private-label, gifting, and wholesale channels, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Chocolate remains a recurring, occasion-linked category, but high penetration limits structural growth. Price elasticity, substitution toward nonchocolate products, health preferences, and retailer assortment pressure offset population and gifting demand, supporting roughly flat constant-price, constant-quality quantity.

caveats: No source measures eligibility within the frozen 43-firm estimate.
The margin bridge used to estimate the band may misclassify branded, retail-heavy, or volatile-margin firms.
e.change_evidence: Classify the 43 frozen candidates using websites, sales channels, customer mix, premises type, ownership, normalized EBITDA, and evidence of repeat B2B programs; management interviews could confirm owner dependence and transferability.
e.rationale: A meaningful share of firms in the code should be recurring wholesale or private-label manufacturers, but the official definition also includes on-premises retail confectioners. Further deductions reflect owner-dependent craft operations, captive facilities, customer concentration, and firms without transferable systems or normalized EBITDA in the target band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311352 ($1,702,247,952) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311352 ($12,908,453,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $395,189 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311352); class <5: 279 firms, avg revenue/firm $285,609 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $43,555 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 209 firms, avg revenue/firm $679,124 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $103,566 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 182 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,377,286 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $210,036 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 200 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,098,675 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $777,548 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $64,205,884 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,791,397 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $618,255,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $94,283,964 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 43

caveats: The estimate excludes storefront-led manufacturers through the lens narrowing.
Consolidation into larger suppliers preserves operator-required demand but may remove it from the LMM population.
o.change_evidence: Make-versus-buy surveys of private-label and wholesale customers, contract-manufacturing churn data, retailer insourcing records, and integration moves by cacao processors would test operator displacement.
o.rationale: Finished confectionery remains a physical, perishable food product requiring an accountable manufacturer for melting, forming, filling, cooling, food safety, packaging, traceability, and delivery. Some customers can insource, source directly from integrated cacao processors, or replace custom manufacturing with standard branded product, so the operator-required share is high but not complete.

q.bridge: Hershey reports pricing and volume for a large branded confectioner, not retention of AI savings by LMM purchased-chocolate converters. Its favorable price realization and elasticity-driven volume declines demonstrate pricing discretion with customer response. I reduce retention for the lens's greater private-label, wholesale, and contract-manufacturing exposure, where rebids and retailer bargaining share savings; Hershey likely overstates retention.
caveats: Large-brand economics do not represent smaller converters.
Observed price realization responds mainly to cocoa and other cost inflation, not AI productivity.
q.change_evidence: Contract clauses, renewal and rebid histories, price-cost bridges, customer concentration, and gross-margin data from private confectioners split by branded, private-label, and contract-manufacturing revenue would materially move q.
q.rationale: Fixed unit and run pricing allows initial retention of productivity benefits, but private-label bids, wholesale renewals, powerful retailers, and easy comparison among converters create medium-term pass-through. Differentiated recipes, service, and seasonal execution support the upper range.

rho.bridge: Census business surveys measure AI adoption and workforce effects across sectors and firm sizes, not implementation of exposed labor tasks in NAICS 311352. I translate rising but size-skewed adoption into five-year implementation, discounting small-plant data quality, food-safety validation, legacy equipment integration, seasonal variability, and change capacity. Broad any-function AI use likely overstates production-grade implementation.
caveats: The BTOS question now covers AI in any business function, broadening measured use.
Survey adoption does not measure the fraction of exposed opportunity actually captured.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor-hour studies for AI planning, vision inspection, maintenance, and customer-order workflows at subscale confectionery plants would directly test implementation rates.
rho.rationale: Order processing, planning, documentation, and customer-service uses can deploy with limited equipment changes; inspection and production optimization are harder. Standardized purchased inputs make implementation somewhat easier than upstream cacao processing, but LMM plants still face capital and validation constraints.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among US business owners across industries: 17% for owners age 55 or older and 10% for younger owners. Project Equity reports 60% of small and midsize manufacturers have owners at or near retirement age. I blend those signals and discount gifts, internal reorganizations, closures, abandoned sales, and businesses too owner-dependent to transfer. Manufacturing age pressure may overstate qualifying completed sales.
caveats: The evidence does not isolate this NAICS or EBITDA band.
Stated plans are not completed transactions and include transfer forms outside the qualifying definition.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-control-transaction records from Axial, GF Data, BizBuySell, or IBBA filtered to confectionery manufacturers and the target EBITDA band, divided by a verified eligible-firm denominator, would materially revise this probability.
s5.rationale: The ownership-age profile creates a credible succession pipeline, but only a subset of planned exits closes as a qualifying control transfer. Customer concentration, seasonal working capital, founder-linked recipes or relationships, and facility financing lower completion.

principal_driver: Recurring chocolate demand and outsourced private-label or wholesale production preserve the need for accountable physical manufacturers.
principal_weakness: Most labor remains physical, while competitive rebids and retailer leverage limit retention of implemented savings.

caveats: S1 is reachable but does not surface the code-specific NAICS definition in its rendered content. This weakens citation traceability for the lens and for e and o, but both primitives are already labeled ESTIMATE and correcting the support status would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The a estimate combines broad NAICS 311 occupational evidence with an all-manufacturing, all-advanced-technology exposure measure; there is no six-digit wage-weighted task evidence. The bridge and likely overstatement direction are disclosed, and a downgrade would not change the base tier or action.

S6 supports little or no overall headcount and skill impact but not the claimed workflow effects. Rho remains an honestly labeled cross-sector proxy, and removing that wording would not change the base tier or action.

The s5 range converts cross-industry owner intentions and an advocacy-source retirement statistic into completed qualifying control-sale probability without transaction evidence. That limitation is disclosed and cannot change the already model-conditioned action; downward correction would leave the base tier STRUCTURAL_OUT.

The q estimate is inferred from a large branded confectioner's inflation-driven pricing rather than LMM converter retention of AI savings. The mismatch is disclosed; even a substantial downward correction to C would not change the base tier because H remains the binding weakest link, nor would it change the action.

S10 does not support the claim that inflation lifted dollars while unit and volume sales struggled, so the d5 constant-price bridge is more judgmental than its prose suggests. Correcting or removing that claim could move D but would not change the base tier or action.

sources_audited.note: The Census NAICS page was reachable, but its rendered content did not expose the 311352 definition or the claimed on-premises retail inclusion, even when reopened with the six-digit query.

The BLS NAICS 311 page supports the broad food-manufacturing scope, the inclusion of on-premises bakery and candy retail production, the large production/nonsupervisory workforce, and common batchmaking and packaging occupations.
BLS supports the cited physical duties, 78% food-manufacturing employment share, automatic weighing and mixing equipment, and 5% projected 2024-2034 employment growth.
Census reports 52% manufacturing-worker exposure versus 28% elsewhere and explicitly characterizes exposure as an upper-bound measure covering advanced automating technologies.
Census reports overall AI use of 17%-20%, expected use of 20%-23%, higher use among larger firms, and the broadened any-business-function wording.
Census supports little or no overall worker-number and skill change after technology adoption, but the packet's additional statement about workflow effects is not established by the cited page.
Gallup supports the fall-2024 five-year plan figures of 17% for owners age 55 or older and 10% for younger owners, while clearly including sales, gifts, and rare public offerings.
Project Equity explicitly reports that 60% of small- and medium-sized manufacturers have owners at or near retirement age.
Hershey's filing supports favorable price realization, price-elasticity-related volume declines, and lags between list-price changes and net-sales effects.

NCA supports $55 billion of 2025 sales, 99.8% household penetration, a $62.2 billion 2030 forecast, chocolate share, and seasonal concentration, but the cited page does not support the packet's assertion that inflation lifted dollars while unit and volume sales struggled.
